parts.answer: • Comment identifying an appropriate safety issue • Associated control measure Examples • Risk of scalding from hot water • Clamp beaker • Risk of burns from hot apparatus • Wear gloves • Risk of electric shock from power supply • Keep separate from water bath Or Use a low voltage power supply (1) (1) Total for question 1 6 Number Answer Mark
• Identifies upthrust = weight (of displaced fluid) • See W = m × g and m = V × ρ • See V = A × d and A = πr • A conversion to SI units (e.g. g to kg) (1) (1) (1) (1)

• Calculates gradient using large triangle • Use of their gradient = 1/πr • Diameter = 6.9 to 7.1 cm Accept use of a correct pair of values from the graph and the equation stated for 1 mark only. Example of calculation gradient = (6.8 cm – 1.6 cm) / 200 g = 0.026 cm g –1 푟푟= � 0.026 휋휋 =3.5 cm diameter = 2 × r = 7.0 cm (1) (1) (1)

• Mass/weight of the beaker (not included) • Add the mass of the beaker to the mass of the load (and plot total) Or subtracting the depth when mass added is 0 (1) (1) Total for question 2 9 Number Answer Mark
• Mass (of solution) obtained using a (top pan) balance • Volume (of solution) measured with a measuring cylinder • Calculate density = mass / volume (ρ = m / V) (1) (1) (1)
• Positive intercept on the refractive index axis • Refractive index increases as mass of salt added increases (1) (1)

• Measure θ for different θ • Measure at least 5 pairs of angles • Plot graph of sin θ against sin θ • Refractive index is the gradient of the line (1) (1) (1) (1) • Use of n sin θ = n sin θ (with n = 1) • Max value = 1.38 • Min value = 1.30 Example of calculation 푛푛 푚푚푚푚푚푚 = sin33.5 ̊ sin23.5 ̊ =1.384 푛푛 푚푚푚푚푚푚 = sin32.5 ̊ sin24.5 ̊ =1.296 (1) (1) (1) • Use of half range of values • Percentage uncertainty = 3 (%) Allow ecf from (d)(i) Example of calculation Range of values = 1.38 – 1.30 = 0.08 Half range of values = 0.04 percentage uncertainty= 0.04 1.34 × 100%=3% (1) (1) Total for question 3 14 Number Answer Mark • Mass is not measured to the nearest gram Or mass is not measured (in kg) to 3 d.p. • Inconsistent/incorrect number of significant figures for GPE • Mean energy supplied values should be 3 s.f. (to match measured values) (Accept 2 d.p.) (1) (1) (1)

• Use of E g = mgh • Change in gravitational potential energy = 0.88 (J) • Mean energy supplied = 3.34 (J) Examples of calculation E g = 0.12 kg × 0.75 m × 9.81N kg –1 = 0.883 J Mean = (3.32 J + 3.36 J + 3.33 J) ÷ 3 = 3.34 J (1) (1) (1)

• Labels axes with quantities and units • Sensible scales • Plotting • Line of best fit Change GPE / J Mean E Supplied / J 0.147 0.573 0.29 1.12 0.441 1.7 0.59 2.23 0.74 2.84 0.88 3.34 (1) (1) (2) (1)
• Calculates gradient using large triangle • Efficiency = 0.25 to 0.27 (accept value converted to %) Example of calculation Gradient = (0.79 J – 0.26 J) ÷ (3.00 J – 1.00 J) = 0.265 (1) (1)

• Continue increasing the mass and extend the graph • Identify the mass/point at which the line starts to curve • Take smaller increments in mass around this point OR • Using larger masses, calculate the efficiency (using efficiency = mgh ÷ mean energy supplied) and plot a graph of efficiency against mass • Identify the mass/point where the graph peaks Or identify the mass where efficiency starts to decrease • Take smaller increments in mass around this point (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 4 15 y = 0.2639x - 0.004 0.00 0.10 0.20 0.30 0.40 0.50 0.60 0.70 0.80 0.90 1.00 0.001.002.003.004.00 Gravitation Potential Energy / J Mean Energy Supplied / J Number Answer Mark

• (Diameter is 1/20 the original) so area is 1/400 original • (For the same breaking stress) maximum force needed to break the sample is only 20N ( so it is safe) Accept correct calculations of both areas (with no comparison) for MP1 Accept repeated/combined calculations using σ = F / A leading to a force of 20N to score both marks. (1) (1)

• Use of W = mg and A = πd /4 • Use of σ = F / A • Breaking stress of sample = 2.62 × 10 ( Pa) Or Force for manufacturers breaking stress = 18.1 (N) • Comparative statement consistent with their value For MP1 accept use of A = πr Example of Calculation W = mg = 1.9 kg × 9.81 N kg –1 = 18.6 N A = πd /4 = π × (0.00095 m) / 4 = 7.1 × 10 –7 m σ = F / A = 18.6 N / 7.1 × 10 –7 m = 2.62 × 10 Pa (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 5 6 Pearson Education Limited.
